Farm Tile Animations — Re-evaluation

A close-up, per-tile audit of all 63 farm seasonal vector tiles (src/textures/seasonal/**) — their four per-season keyframes, the two-tier "WarCraft-III" idle, and the season→season transitions — rendered faithfully on the real board disc. Re-scored under a uniqueness-first lens: a singular, lively, bold-and-fun tile beats a polished one that repeats a shared idiom.

Re-evaluation · 2026-06-23 · scope: farm only (fish, minerals, coin & pearl excluded) · vector pipeline, not PixelLab · 63 tiles, 10 families

63 farm tiles 29 at bar (≥4.0) 34 below 4.0 18 produce share one bounce 26 animals share one scarf close-up + source review

Overview

The seasonal vector set is technically excellent: the two-tier idle machinery is provably seamless (anim(t=0) equals the resting still, gestures return to rest with zero value and velocity) and the season→season transitions hit exact endpoints. The land creatures — birds, herd, cattle, mounts — are the high point: distinct bodies, characterful rare actions, and bold fantasy beats (phoenix fire-burst, mammoth trumpet, triceratops charge).

The re-evaluation flips one axis. This pass scores uniqueness over consistency, per the brief: a tile that repeats a shared idiom is marked down, not up. Under that lens two systemic problems dominate the farm, and they are exactly the two you flagged:
  • One bounce, 18 produce. Every fruit and vegetable animates with the identical side-to-side wobble + squash-stretch "hop." It is competent and seamless — but a hopping apple is arbitrary, and seeing the same hop on all 18 is the opposite of per-tile character. This single issue accounts for 18 of the 34 sub-4 tiles.
  • One scarf, 26 animals. Every bird/herd/cattle/mount wears the same knitted winter scarf. The animals survive it (distinct bodies + rare actions carry them), but the prop itself is "meh by repetition."
TL;DR. 29 of 63 farm tiles are at bar (≥4.0) and the character-animation craft is real. The 34 below the bar cluster into a short, fixable list: the produce-bounce monoculture (18), a handful of winter "white-out" keyframes (oak, birch, willow), three cross-filed identity tiles (heather, clover, melon), a few weak/absent rares (grass, boar, wild-goose, rice) and one correctness bug (longhorn's head detaches mid-swing). The Plan → ≥4 tab details the work tile-by-tile.

Overall-score distribution (63 farm tiles, uniqueness-first)

Direct answers to the brief

Are they lively / interesting enough?

The animals: yes — genuinely lively, two-tier, characterful. The produce: not really — the stills are beautiful but the motion is one generic hop, so they read "decorated" rather than "alive." Plain grass is the least lively tile in the set.

Uniqueness vs. a consistent repeat?

Two repeats dominate and both should go: the produce bounce (18 tiles) and the winter scarf (26 tiles). Lesser repeats: one robin bird on 5 of 6 trees, and a 4-strong swine cluster (pig/hog/boar/warthog). Uniqueness is now the call — fall back to a shared idiom only where nothing better fits (e.g. the wind idle genuinely suits grain & grass).

The WC3 rare — bold & fun?

For creatures, frequently yes (turkey tail-fan, pig wallow, phoenix burst). For produce it is a bigger bounce — the same motion, not a bold beat. And a few creatures have a rare that never separates from the idle (boar, wild-goose, longhorn, rice).

Per-season keyframes — keep or change?

Mostly keep — the four-season stills are the strongest layer. Change winter on oak/birch/willow (snow buries the subject) and fix identity on heather/clover/melon. Palm is the model: winter frosts, it never whites out.

How this was evaluated

Each tile was baked through the real board pipeline (paintTileCanvas: the rounded-disc radial gradient from TILE_CATEGORY_COLORS, then the origin-centred seasonal art) at high resolution, so what you see is what the board shows. A motion-energy scan samples each idle 0–18 s and auto-isolates the two tiers (the calm common beat and the rare action). Every tile got a close-up zoom pass plus a source read of its poseFromClock / transition code.

One caveat on the scan. The "WC3 crest" frame is the global motion-energy maximum. For tiles with continuous motion (trees swaying, grass, geese in flight) that peak is just a sway extreme, not a designed rare action — so for those the rare tier was judged from the source, not the frame. The filmstrips label the auto-detected times; read them with that in mind.

Seasonal keyframes — keep them, with three fixes

The four-season stills are the strongest layer of the whole set: legible silhouettes, distinct per-season palettes, and tasteful seasonal dressing (blossom→gloss→leaf-fall→snow). For most tiles the answer to "should we change the keyframes?" is no — keep them. Three specific problems are worth fixing.

1 · The winter "white-out" — the one recurring keyframe defect

On the deciduous trees the winter snow-load is tuned so heavy that it buries the subject instead of frosting it. Oak and birch become a white starburst; willow's signature weeping curtain is deleted (leafDensity drops to 0.2) and replaced by a floating snow bar — "a different plant." The evergreens prove the right recipe: keep the body visible and cap it with snow.

The fix is local and low-risk. Halve the snow-stroke widths on oak/birch limbs and darken the bare-branch colour so it reads between the ridges; raise willow's winter leafDensity to ~0.55 and frost the surviving strands. No silhouette redraw needed.

2 · Identity mis-reads — three tiles render as the wrong thing

Three tiles are cross-filed — the art and the registry key disagree, so the tile reads as the wrong object in the collection:

  • heather (tile_grass_heather) is a flower drawing under a grass key — it reads as a generic purple flower, neither heather nor grass.
  • clover (tile_bird_clover) is a clover patch under a bird key — ground cover, not a creature.
  • melon (tile_bird_melon) is a melon (produce) under a bird key — and it even inherits the produce bounce.

None is a rendering bug — each draws fine — but each is mis-identified relative to its slot. Either commit the silhouette to the keyed identity or re-home the tile to the right family.

What's working — the keyframe exemplars

Palm is the template: tropical winter is lightly frosted with the fronds still green, and autumn drops a whole frond and a coconut on the pad — the clearest seasonal storytelling in the set. Apple, lemon, rambutan, beet, turnip, mushroom, ram, cow all have four genuinely distinct, well-crafted season stills. The produce problem is not the keyframes — it's the motion (next tab).

The two-tier idle — and the "bold & fun" bar

The design is a WarCraft-III-style two-tier idle: a calm common beat every ~6 s, and a rare bold action every ~12–18 s, stitched so the loop never pops. The machinery is excellent. The question this pass asks is sharper: is the rare action actually bold and fun? — a beat you'd be delighted to catch, not merely a bigger version of the idle.

What "bold & fun" looks like — the bar to hold

The creatures clear this bar often: hen lay/flap, pig wallow, warthog charge, phoenix fire-burst, mammoth trumpet-stomp, triceratops horn-lower charge, the tree snow-plops (cypress/fir). These are characterful and legible at a glance.

The produce "rare" is a bigger bounce — not a bold beat

For all 18 fruit & veg, the rare action is — per the source's own comment — "the bounce is the show": a squash-stretch hop. It's competently animated and seamless, but it is the same motion as the idle, only bigger, and a hopping apple isn't expressive of what an apple is.

Bold ≠ big. The turkey gains character by doing something a turkey does (fanning its tail). The apple gains nothing by hopping higher. The lift for produce isn't "more bounce" — it's a different, fruit-specific beat (see the Plan).

Where the rare tier is weak or absent

A few creatures have a rare that never separates from the common (its motion energy barely exceeds the idle), so the two-tier promise doesn't land:

  • boar — rare ≈ common (e=0.69); the lunge reads as just more chewing. rework
  • longhorn — head-swing barely separates (e=0.93) and the head detaches mid-swing. P0 bug
  • wild-goose — continuous flight, no discrete bold beat (e≈1).
  • rice — the gust-bend barely rises above the rustle (e=0.72).
  • plain grass — essentially no rare at all; the least lively tile in the set.

Uniqueness > repetition

Per the brief, this pass treats a shared idiom as a liability, not a consistency win — fall back to a repeat only where nothing better fits. Four repeats run through the farm, in rough order of how much they cost.

A · One bounce — 18 produce tiles

Every fruit and vegetable shares the same wobble + squash-stretch hop. At the rare-action apex they are nearly interchangeable:

This is the #1 fix: the Plan proposes a distinct, in-character beat for each fruit and vegetable, keeping the bounce only as a last-resort fallback.

B · One scarf — 26 animals

Every bird, herd animal, cow and mount wears the same knitted winter scarf. The animals are individually strong, so it doesn't sink them — but the prop is "meh by repetition," exactly as you called it.

C · Lesser repeats

One robin bird, 5 of 6 trees

Oak, birch, cypress, fir and willow all draw the identical #5a4636/#d2693a robin as their rare flit; only palm varies it. Cheap win — tint the bird per species.

The swine cluster (4 tiles)

Pig, hog, boar and warthog read close in body and motion. Pig and warthog earn their place with strong distinct rares; hog and boar need a differentiated silhouette and beat.

The wind idle (grain + grass)

Nine tiles share a rustle + gust-bend. This one is fine — wind through a field is the right motion for grain and grass, and the brief explicitly allows a shared idiom where nothing better fits. Keep it; just strengthen the weaker rares.

The chew idle (ruminants)

Cow, longhorn, sheep, goat, ram and alpaca share a cud-chew common beat. Like the wind, this is appropriate behaviour, not lazy reuse — keep it.

The principle. A shared idiom is fine when it expresses what the subject does (wind on grain, chewing on a cow). It's a problem when it's arbitrary (a hopping apple) or purely decorative (one scarf for all). Fix the arbitrary repeats; keep the meaningful ones.

Per-tile scores — all 63 farm tiles

Every farm tile, grouped by family and ranked by overall within its family. Each card shows the tile's actual render — its four season keyframes + the idle crest + the WC3 (rare) crest on the real board disc — with its four axis scores and the specific work to reach ≥4. Axes: KF seasonal keyframes · Idle common beat · WC3 rare action (bold&fun) · Uniq uniqueness (singular vs. shared idiom). Overall leans on WC3 + Uniq.

Filter:

Trees 6 tiles · avg 3.9 · 3 below 4

5.0 Palm tile_tree_palm exemplar at bar
KF 5Idle 5WC3 5Uniq 5 idle 6.7s · WC3 12.7s · e 0.98
The template. Tropical winter is genuinely frosted, not snowed-under — fronds stay green; autumn drops a whole frond AND a coconut on the pad; the common idle is a true traveling-wave breeze; and it is the only tree that varies its bird. Cleanest architecture in the set (paint(p) is both still and transition endpoint).
4.0 Cypress tile_tree_cypress shared bird at bar
KF 4Idle 4WC3 5Uniq 3 idle 6.5s · WC3 0.5s · e 1
Deep-green spire stays clearly visible under capped snow (correct frosted winter), and the rare snow-plop — a clump lets go of the lowest bough, falls, and the load re-settles — is a model bold beat. Held back by the shared robin bird and evergreen seasons that read similar.
To reach ≥4 → Vary the bird per species; enlarge the spring new-growth candles / autumn cones.
4.0 Fir tile_tree_fir shared bird at bar
KF 4Idle 4WC3 5Uniq 3 idle 12.6s · WC3 6.5s · e 1
Excellent rare snow-clump-plop (shaves the tier's load as the clump tumbles, re-settles by q=1). The heaviest evergreen winter — green still peeks at the tier fringes but it edges toward a frosted wedding-cake. Shares the robin bird.
To reach ≥4 → Trim the winter crest width ~20% so more green shows; vary the bird; give autumn a stronger olive needle cast.
3.5 Oak tile_tree_oak winter white-outshared bird
KF 3Idle 4WC3 4Uniq 3 idle 6.5s · WC3 0.5s · e 1
Spring blossoms, lush summer and the best autumn fire-ramp in the set are gorgeous — but the winter snow-load is tuned so heavy it buries the bare branches into a white starburst. Also: autumn anim(t=0)≠draw (a leaf-jump on loop entry), and oak alone crossfades its transitions (ghosting mid-morph).
To reach ≥4 → Halve the winter snow-stroke widths so the dark branch reads between ridges; fix the autumn loop seam; port transitions to lerpP; vary the bird.
3.5 Willow tile_tree_willow winter white-out
KF 3Idle 5WC3 3Uniq 4 idle 12.6s · WC3 6.6s · e 0.97
The traveling-wave weeping curtain is the most beautiful common idle in the set — a real willow drift. But winter sets leafDensity 0.2 and deletes the curtain, leaving a bare post under a floating snow bar — 'a different plant.' The rare is only the generic bird-flit.
To reach ≥4 → Raise winter leafDensity to ~0.55 and frost/snow-cap the surviving strands; give it a characterful rare; vary the bird.
3.2 Birch tile_tree_birch winter white-outshared bird
KF 3Idle 4WC3 3Uniq 3 idle 12.5s · WC3 0.5s · e 0.99
White snow on near-white bark on a pale pad → in winter the slender-birch identity (white trunk + lenticels) vanishes into a white fan — the worst white-on-white in the set. Summer crown barely shows the trunk; catkins read as tiny ticks; the bird-flit rare is timid.
To reach ≥4 → Darken the winter bare-branch stroke and thin the snow; open the summer crown (lower blob alpha); bolder catkins; a livelier rare; vary the bird.

Fruit 9 tiles · avg 3.3 · 9 below 4

3.7 Coconut tile_fruit_coconut shared bounce
KF 5Idle 4WC3 3Uniq 3 idle 6.5s · WC3 15.6s · e 0.38
The one fruit that breaks the bounce mold — a heavy palm-like rock plus a sprout-tuft sway, a fallen frond in autumn, and the coconut clearly visible under winter snow. Still wobble-family, so it stops just short of a characterful rare.
To reach ≥4 → Give it a true rare beat (a coconut splits and water drips) and it clears 4.
3.6 Rambutan tile_fruit_rambutan shared bounce
KF 5Idle 3WC3 3Uniq 3 idle 0.5s · WC3 15.5s · e 0.39
A striking hairy-red exotic still — one of the most distinctive looks on the board — wasted on the shared wobble + squash-stretch hop. The rare is the same generic bounce as every other fruit.
To reach ≥4 → Replace the hop with a rambutan-specific beat: the hairs bristle / a spike flicks / it rolls to show its pale flesh.
3.6 Starfruit tile_fruit_starfruit shared bounce
KF 5Idle 3WC3 3Uniq 3 idle 0.5s · WC3 15.6s · e 0.41
Lovely translucent star cross-section; identical wobble + bounce motion to the rest of produce.
To reach ≥4 → A slow rotate-to-reveal-the-star, or a translucent light shimmer, instead of the hop.
3.4 Apple tile_fruit_apple shared bounce
KF 5Idle 3WC3 3Uniq 2 idle 0.5s · WC3 15.5s · e 0.29
Beautiful four-season stills — green apple on spring grass → glossy red summer → autumn leaf on the pad → red apple on a snow mound. But the idle is the generic side-to-side wobble and the 'rare' is the same squash-stretch hop on every other fruit and vegetable. Competent and seamless, but a hopping apple is arbitrary, not character.
To reach ≥4 → A worm pokes out of the apple and ducks back; or an apple-specific blossom-flurry / leaf-shed as the rare.
3.4 Lemon tile_fruit_lemon shared bounce
KF 5Idle 3WC3 3Uniq 2 idle 0.5s · WC3 15.5s · e 0.37
Clean glossy citrus with good seasonal palette; the shared wobble + hop.
To reach ≥4 → A citrus-spritz shiver / juice-mist puff as the rare; a faster nervous wobble suits a small fruit.
3.3 Jackfruit tile_fruit_jackfruit shared bounce
KF 4Idle 3WC3 3Uniq 3 idle 0.3s · WC3 15.6s · e 0.33
Big knobbly distinctive silhouette; generic hop that doesn't suit its mass.
To reach ≥4 → A heavy slow wobble for its weight, or a segment-split reveal of the pods.
3.2 Pear tile_fruit_pear shared bounce
KF 4Idle 3WC3 3Uniq 2 idle 0.5s · WC3 15.6s · e 0.52
Solid stills; the shared wobble + bounce.
To reach ≥4 → A top-heavy pendulum sway (the pear shape begs for it) instead of the symmetric hop.
3.0 Golden Apple tile_fruit_golden_apple shared bounce
KF 3Idle 3WC3 3Uniq 3 idle 0.5s · WC3 15.6s · e 0.34
The 'gold' barely reads as special next to a normal red apple, and it inherits the shared hop — so the legendary item feels ordinary.
To reach ≥4 → Push the gold (metallic specular) and add a radiant shine-sweep / sparkle rare to sell the rarity.
2.8 Blackberry tile_fruit_blackberry shared bounce
KF 3Idle 3WC3 3Uniq 2 idle 0.5s · WC3 15.5s · e 0.22
The clustered drupelets read muddy / dark at board size, and it does the same hop as everything else.
To reach ≥4 → Brighten and separate the drupelets; a dew-drip or a berry-plump rare that suits a cluster.

Cross-filed: a clover patch drawn under a bird key. Charming, but in the collection it reads as ground cover, not a bird — mild identity confusion.
To reach ≥4 → Re-home to grass, or commit to a creature; sharpen the read either way.

To reach ≥4 → Add a discrete bold beat above the cruise — a steep banking turn or a call-and-honk.

KF 4Idle 4WC3 3Uniq 3 idle 3.4s · WC3 15.4s · e 0.93
Bug: the head-swing rare detaches the head from the body mid-swing (a correctness defect), and the swing barely separates from the chew (e=0.93). The wide-horn read is otherwise good.
To reach ≥4 → Fix the head-detach (z-order/pivot); strengthen the swing amplitude so the rare reads.

The plan — bring every farm tile to ≥4

34 of 63 tiles sit below 4.0. They fall into eight themes. Tackle them in this order — the first three buy the most quality per unit of work, and the first is by far the biggest lever.

Sequencing.
  1. P0 correctness — the longhorn head-detach and the autumn loop-seam (oak/willow/birch). Real defects; fix first.
  2. The produce motion program — 18 tiles, one shared problem. The single biggest quality win on the board.
  3. Winter white-outs & identity — 6 tiles, all low-risk keyframe edits.
  4. Bold-up the weak rares + diversify the shared props — the long tail.

P0 · Correctness bugs (locatable)

BUG longhorn head detaches

The rare head-swing separates the head from the body mid-swing (z-order/pivot). cattle/longhorn.ts — fix the pivot so the head stays attached; strengthen the swing.

SEAM autumn anim(t=0) ≠ draw

Oak and willow (and birch) render ambient leaf-fall in the anim but not the still, so leaves jump on loop entry. Seed the still at the anim's t=0 phase. tree/oak.ts, willow.ts, birch.ts

POLISH oak crossfade transitions

Oak alone alpha-blends two full draws (ghosting mid-morph) instead of tweening params. Port to the lerpP pattern the other trees use.

P1 · The produce-motion program — the #1 lever

18 of the 34 sub-4 tiles are fruit & veg with beautiful stills and one shared bounce. Give each a distinct, in-character beat (common idle and/or rare); keep the bounce only where nothing better fits. The stills already carry KF — this is purely a motion upgrade, so each tile jumps from ~3.0–3.7 to 4+.

TileNowTargetWork to do
Blackberry
tile_fruit_blackberry		2.8→ 4.0Brighten and separate the drupelets; a dew-drip or a berry-plump rare that suits a cluster.
Broccoli
tile_veg_broccoli		2.9→ 4.0Brighten/clump the florets for legibility; a tremble-and-settle rare.
Carrot
tile_veg_carrot		2.9→ 4.0A pull-up-from-the-soil rare and a top-flick idle.
Squash
tile_veg_squash		2.9→ 4.0Stronger ribbing + specular to sell the form; a heavy slow wobble for its mass.
Golden Apple
tile_fruit_golden_apple		3.0→ 4.0Push the gold (metallic specular) and add a radiant shine-sweep / sparkle rare to sell the rarity.
Pear
tile_fruit_pear		3.2→ 4.0A top-heavy pendulum sway (the pear shape begs for it) instead of the symmetric hop.
Eggplant
tile_veg_eggplant		3.2→ 4.0A glossy jiggle + a calyx (stem-cap) creak that fits the heavy shape.
Pepper
tile_veg_pepper		3.2→ 4.0A heat-shimmer + an internal seed-rattle as the rare.
Cucumber
tile_veg_cucumber		3.2→ 4.0A length-wise bend/roll instead of a vertical hop.
Jackfruit
tile_fruit_jackfruit		3.3→ 4.0A heavy slow wobble for its weight, or a segment-split reveal of the pods.
Apple
tile_fruit_apple		3.4→ 4.0A worm pokes out of the apple and ducks back; or an apple-specific blossom-flurry / leaf-shed as the rare.
Lemon
tile_fruit_lemon		3.4→ 4.0A citrus-spritz shiver / juice-mist puff as the rare; a faster nervous wobble suits a small fruit.
Beet
tile_veg_beet		3.4→ 4.0A soil-shrug that bares the root, or a leaf-rustle, instead of the hop.
Turnip
tile_veg_turnip		3.4→ 4.0A root-wiggle / greens-flick rare that suits a root vegetable.
Mushroom
tile_veg_mushroom		3.5→ 4.0A spore-puff release as the rare and a gentle cap-breathe idle would be unmistakably mushroom.
Rambutan
tile_fruit_rambutan		3.6→ 4.0Replace the hop with a rambutan-specific beat: the hairs bristle / a spike flicks / it rolls to show its pale flesh.
Starfruit
tile_fruit_starfruit		3.6→ 4.0A slow rotate-to-reveal-the-star, or a translucent light shimmer, instead of the hop.
Coconut
tile_fruit_coconut		3.7→ 4.5Give it a true rare beat (a coconut splits and water drips) and it clears 4.

P2 · The long tail — props & polish

Differentiate the swine cluster (1 tiles)

TileNowTargetWork to do

TileNowTargetWork to do
Alpaca
tile_herd_alpaca		3.7→ 4.5Sharpen the seasonal stills (fleece/coat detail, crisper winter).

Shared-prop diversification (raises the ceiling across many tiles)

  • Winter scarf → winter wardrobe. Vary the accessory across the 26 animals: colour/knit/drape per species, and swap some for earmuffs, a holly sprig, a snow-dusting, or breath-fog only. No tile drops below 4 for this, but it lifts the whole winter row from "samey" to "characterful."
  • Robin bird → per-species bird. Tint/reshape the tree bird per species (palm already does this). Cheap, and it removes the most visible tree repeat.

Where this lands

Every item above is scoped to clear 4.0, and the exemplars (palm, mammoth, the strong birds & mounts) are already there as templates. The work is concentrated, not spread: fix one shared motion (produce), one shared prop (scarf), three winter keyframes, three identity tiles, and a handful of weak rares, and the farm reads uniformly lively and unmistakably per-tile.

Re-evaluation note. Several tiles that scored 4.5–5 in the first pass (most produce) are marked down here — not because they got worse, but because the bar changed: under "uniqueness & bold-fun," a gorgeous still on a generic shared motion is a 3, not a 5. That's the point of the re-score, and it's where the work is.

Farm tile animation re-evaluation · 63 tiles · uniqueness-first · rendered on the real board pipeline. Fish, minerals, coin & pearl out of scope.
